publius_sulpicius_convo_1_n01.npcDialogue: The vote passed. The Mithridatic command belongs to Marius — legally, constitutionally, beyond appeal. You should have seen the optimates' faces. Like men reading their own obituaries. But here is where I stop celebrating, Crispus, because my informants at Nola tell me Sulla's legions have broken camp. Not disbanded — broken camp. Marching formation. We have perhaps five days before Roman soldiers cross the pomerium to murder Roman law. So I will ask you once, plainly: are you ready for what that means?

options.playerDialogue: I'm ready. Tell me what needs doing before he reaches the gates.
Is there still a way to stop this? Some arrangement — some deal that keeps the legions on the road?
Five days. I need to get my family out of Rome first — then I'm yours entirely.

publius_sulpicius_convo_1_n02.npcDialogue: Three tasks, and none of them pleasant. First — the urban plebs need to be armed. Not swords, not spears — roof tiles, paving stones, boiling water. If Sulla enters street by street, every tenement becomes a fortress. Second — the senate moderates need reminding that submission to a marching army makes them all accomplices to treason. Third — and I say this with great affection for the old man — someone needs to find Marius and tell him to stop drinking his wine and start earning his legend.

options.playerDialogue: Which one is most likely to collapse without attention in the next twelve hours?

publius_sulpicius_convo_1_n03.npcDialogue: A deal. Think about what you're saying, Crispus — really think. We just stripped the man's command through a lawful vote of the sovereign assembly. Put yourself behind his eyes for one moment. Would you negotiate? The only deal Sulla accepts is total surrender — rescind the vote, exile me, deliver Marius in chains. Is that what you're proposing? Because if not, stop looking for doors that don't exist.

responses.playerDialogue: No. The vote was lawful. We don't hand constitutional process to a man with legions simply because he has legions.
What if we split it — Sulla keeps his eastern command, Marius receives a separate commission? Give them both what they want.
You're right — there's nothing to negotiate. Let him come. We'll make him bleed for every street.
Send envoys anyway — not to negotiate, to delay. Every day we buy with talk is a day we spend building barricades.

fallbackResponse.playerDialogue: I don't know. But someone should at least try before the blood starts.

publius_sulpicius_convo_1_n04.npcDialogue: Your family — yes. That's not cowardice, that's arithmetic. When the proscription lists go up, the families go first — wives, children, anyone whose suffering might make a wanted man come crawling back. Get them south. Not Capua — gods, not Capua, that's Sulla's staging ground. Brundisium. By sea if you can arrange it. And then, Crispus — then you come back to me. Because I need you here when the world inverts.

options.playerDialogue: They'll be on the road by dawn. Now — what's the plan for the city?

publius_sulpicius_convo_1_n05.npcDialogue: The senate. Half those men will fold like wet papyrus the instant they see eagles on the Appian Way. I need you to visit three of them tonight — Scaevola, Cinna, Antonius. Look them in the eye and explain — slowly, since they're senators — that bowing to Sulla's march means endorsing the principle that any general with enough swords can rewrite the law. Make them understand that if they bend now, there is no Republic left to straighten.

options.playerDialogue: I'll see all three before sunrise. What argument breaks through to each of them?
Forget the senators — they'll talk until the swords are at their throats. Arm the plebs. The streets decide this, not the curia.

publius_sulpicius_convo_1_n06.npcDialogue: Delay tactics. Shared commands. Compromises. These are the strategies of a man who has not yet accepted that the ground beneath him has already shifted. But I don't fault you for it — acceptance takes most people longer than they have.

publius_sulpicius_convo_1_n07.npcDialogue: Your work with the senators bought us time — not certainty, never certainty, but time. And now that time is spent. Word from the Esquiline — a column of Sulla's advance scouts, sighted on the road. This is no longer rhetoric, Crispus. This is iron and hobnails. I'm going to the rostra to speak to the people one last time — while the rostra still belongs to the people. Will you stand beside me on the platform?

options.playerDialogue: At your shoulder. Let them see two men who aren't afraid — even if one of them is lying about it.
I'm more useful in the crowd — organizing, directing. You give the speech. I'll make sure Rome hears it.

publius_sulpicius_convo_1_n08.npcDialogue: The streets, then. Very well — the people of Rome have never seen legions turned against their own walls. They are terrified, and terrified men either freeze or they fight. The difference is usually one voice at the right moment. I need you in the Subura when the first cohorts push through. Can you look a crowd of frightened butchers and tanners in the eye and turn them into a wall?

options.playerDialogue: Give me your freedmen network and six hours. I'll have barricades on every corner of the Subura and men behind each one.
Barricades won't stop legions, Sulpicius — they'll slow them for an hour and then the killing starts. We need to get you and Marius out of the city before Sulla seals the gates.

publius_sulpicius_convo_1_n09.npcDialogue: Good. Then whatever happens in the next three days — whatever they do to us — history will record that some Romans looked at the legions and did not kneel. That matters, Crispus. Especially if we lose. Now go — I have one last speech to write, and it needs to be the kind men remember. We'll drink together when this is over. Or they'll pour the wine on our graves and wonder if we were brave or merely stupid.

publius_sulpicius_convo_1_n10.npcDialogue: Perhaps you're right. Perhaps the Republic dies today and all my cleverness amounts to a footnote. But footnotes survive, Crispus — that's the thing about them. Get word to Marius — the harbor at Ostia is still open, though not for long. If we can't fight, we vanish, and we come back when the moment turns. Go quickly. Sulla's outriders are already at the Colline Gate, and they are not here to negotiate.

deltas.reason: Stood publicly against Sulla's illegal march
Sulla's proscription lists include your name
The Italians respect those who resist tyranny
Defied armed legions in the streets of Rome
Helped Marian leaders escape Sulla's advance
Sulla's agents note your involvement in the evacuation
Chose survival over martyrdom when the cause was lost
